speaker: interviewer
text: January 25, 1996. Survivor Kaz Wolff-Zdzienicki. Interviewer Padigail Meskin. City of Byrne, Village, Natal. Country, South Africa. Language, English. Kaz, we're now at your liberation. You've seen the American army. Can you tell me what happens from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was, I would say, in the army. I was-- then they, you see, there was, there were some SS division in the Mecklenburg Forest. And the army decided to clean the forest from the SS division who refused to sur-- to surrender. So they formed couple of companies. Well, it was rather on the volunteer basis. I joined, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this the American army or the Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: American [? American ?].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, and during this cleaning of the forest, I was wounded. And then I I was in the hospital. I was partially paralyzed from the waist down, because this was a mine who break down behind me. But anyhow, I received couple of months of rehabilitation holiday. I received a choice where I would like to spend this holiday. This had been a rehabilitation period. I decided to go to a former pris-- Polish prisoner of war camp. I was there. And one day, I decided-- in beginning '46 in winter-- to see what's going on in south of Germany, because I was in the area of Cologne, of [INAUDIBLE], Kiel, on north. Weather unpleasant-- cold, wet, misty, so I went to Munich. And I started working at Munich. I was still on, on leave. And I went to the officer's casino for a dinner or something like that. And then I saw French officers there. So I started to talk to them. They told me what they are doing there, that they are from the French prosecution of war criminals, and they are looking for a-- if-- they ask me if I speak German. And I said, well, I speak fluently German.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, well, they realized that I speak fluently French. So they say, why you don't join us to be a interpreter? I say, I say, OK. I have nothing again, but I must have a permission from my authorities. They say, don't worry. We will arrange this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they have done this very quick, maybe-- I don't know. In maybe one week time, I receive an order sent from Frankfurt-- because there was a general-- was a headquarter of General Eisenhower, and afterwards of General Clay, who was commanding general of the American occupation zone in Germany-- that I am, I have to join the prosecution and judge division of the US Army for war criminals and report to a village, Dachau, which was south of Munich, between Munich and Garmisch-Partenkirchen, beautiful ski resort there, to the commanding officer, I remember Colonel Fainter, to start my duty there. And Dachau was a former big, really, first German concentration camp far before the war, the oldest one. But when I arrived there, it was the springtime '46. There was full of 41,000 SS and war criminals. There was a special camp, for example, where you have the generals and ministers and the top Gestapo people with Marshal Milch of the air force, with Kesselring who was marshal in charge of the army in Italy, who was plenty of-- I remember a personal doctor of Hitler, and so on, Gestapo people, Gestapo Muller, the famous one-- infamous one, and so on and so on. And for the beginning, I was put in charge of security of this general camp. There was an administrative officer. It was an American sergeant. It was a doctor, American captain, a doctor, chief doctor. Because it was together with a hospital camp combined. And I was, I say, in charge of security. So I had guards all around. You know, I have to check their post, reading incoming letters and outgoing letters. And well, anyhow, keep them under control. And as well, I had after-- I had to go to the prison to interrogate. For the beginning, I was interpreting between the French and the prisoner.

speaker: interviewee
text: But afterwards, they realized that I got some idea about interrogation. And they could not lie to me. Like, for example, Boger wanted to lie to me, explaining me, convincing me that he wasn't first on far in Russia, in a administrative job. Then, when I say, well, it was, Russia? It was Poland, I say. He changed his mind and say, yes, in Poland. Then he admitted that he was in administration in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: But eventually, under pressure, he admitted that he was-- how we'd call it in Deputy Chief of [INAUDIBLE]. It means the Gestapo for the concentration camp. It was a mur-- he was a murderer. He was a biggest murderer, maybe, in the, in the camp. And he arrived to the prison, internal prison, in the camp. He instructed to send 10, for example, people outside. He had a little carbine with him. He ordered the people to run around. And he was shooting into their heads. It was for fun. It was for fun. Anyhow, and then they-- of course, I interrogated them. Some of their names I forgot already. Well, some have been very clever, like for the Gestapo Muller, who spoke fluently a couple of languages, including French. So we interrogated him in English, in French, in German. And he was always, you see, like a snake, avoiding answers and this-- a clever chap. Anyhow, and then well, different people. Boger, and then was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Milch.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ilse Koch.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The wife of commandant of Buchenwald. You know, this is a woman who had a hobby to collect tattoo skin, human skin, to make lampshade, to make albums cover, books cover, and so on. And the story started like that, that I was a witness in her court case. Because when they prepared the courtroom for her process, I walked in by coincidence. And I saw a beautiful lampshade with a bunch of roses on from human skin. And I recognized this.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a young German who had-- was with me in Auschwitz and had on his left arm this beautiful bunch of roses tattooed-- leaf greens, green leaves and red flower-- three or four, I don't remember now. And I recognized this. And on this was a ticket, you know? Written on the ticket was the, was a, how you call it-- a unknown. On some of them wasn't. For example, she had a arm, a piece of skin from the arm with a number of Auschwitz. So it was easy to find out who it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in this case, I say-- I ran to the prosecutor. I say, listen, old chap! I know this chap! I know it was a German prisoner, political prisoner, a German one. I remember that he was in-- he told me, when they ask him why he's in the army in the concentration camp, he told me that he was in a scouting movement before. And as you maybe know, they-- Hitler arrested all the top people from the scouting when he came to power. Because scouting was a-- I would say, competition to his Hitlerjugend movement. So he had to destroy the scouting. Anyhow, so it was, you know-- and I, I have done this for couple of-- well, more than one year, interrogating the people. Well, they're different-- some small, not especially famous, you know, some small guard from concentration camp. But you, you know, from guard, you go to NCO, from NCO to officer, from the officer to the commanding officer. And you collect the accusation for somebody else from the interrogation of the chap at the bottom. I must admit that they-- generally, they have been singing like birds. I remember when I was arrested, and my colleagues in Warsaw, we refused to give answer. We distorted the answer. We gave a cover-up for somebody who was arrested or maybe was not arrested but was free still. The German, under pressure, gave everything about everybody what you ask. Very few, very few, very seldom, you have to use, I would say, a harder pressure on them. They have been soft like hell. Of course, you see, today, when you go to Germany and you see people my age and older, nobody was not aware. Nobody was aware of concentration camp. They don't know what was Gestapo. But if you see the film from this time, you see thousands of people greeting Hitler, Himler, and the whole top people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kaz, I'd love to just ask you a few questions. And then as we go, we'll develop the story. Can you please tell me the names, if you can remember, of the trials that you were involved with? Which ones can you remember? You mentioned Colonel Milch.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was Ilse Koch for sure. There was a problem of the extradition of Boger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry. Before we go-- Ilse Koch, were you, were you an interrogator for her or just a witness?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ilse Koch?

speaker: interviewer
text: For Ilse, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: A witness.

speaker: interviewer
text: A witness. OK. Because of the lamp shade?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And then for Colonel Boger? Could you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Boger, yes. I interrogated Boger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us how-- what happened there? Because I know there was an extradition, et cetera.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, Boger supposed to be put before the court in-- in Dachau or Munich or Nuremberg. But due to the fact that Auschwitz, where he was mainly in his job, was in Poland, there was, for me, a possibility to turn the story this way that he should stay before the Polish court. I was aware once he came back to Poland, he will be hanged straightaway. There was no other. And then, he was put in a transport with other war criminals, with, for example, the chief, cruel chief of women's camp, Maria Mandl, as well was in this, in this transport, and so on, couple of other officers and so on. But he managed to escape when the train passed Czechoslovakia. Boger escaped and come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for I remember, he was from Stuttgart. He came back, and he went before the war in the police. So he came back to his place of living in West Germany. And only after two or three years, they find out him. Because he was still on the list of war criminal who they were searching for.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he was arrested. Well, he put, was put before a court in Frankfurt. And due to the fact that Germany, quick, quick, after the war, abolish the death sentence, well, he received life. And that's all

speaker: interviewer
text: You also spoke about Colonel Milch, Colonel Milch.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not colonel-- marshal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, sorry-- Marshal Milch.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Marshal Milch. Marshal Milch was-- I was not interrogating him, because he was not really a specially war criminal. But I had a clash with him during the period when I was security officer of this generals-- of the marshals', generals' camp. He came to me one day, reported not to me, but to the administrative sergeant of the hospital. I don't know what he wanted. Anyhow, and the other was busy. So he started talking with me. And we spoke, of course, German, of course. I was speaking. Of course, I was knowing who he was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he asked me what I was doing during the war, and this and that. And I say that I was in Poland in the underground. Ah, you are part of the banditen! I say, what you say? You call American officer a bandit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he looked. I say, OK. Wait here. I call the guard. I say, take him to prison. I, I sent him to prison. I phoned the chap who was my friend in charge of the prison. I say, well, listen. Give Milch couple of days' hard time. And he was put on bread and water for a couple of days. And couple of days passed. And he came back and have to report to me that he is back from prison. Now, then he came. He reported himself to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, well, Milch, so who I was during the war? Was I a bandit? He said, oh, no, lieutenant. Oh, no. You were a hero! [LAUGHS] He changed his mind completely. You were a hero! [LAUGHS] OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, I-- one day, I was in Dusseldorf in the wine shop. And I, I was with a German doctor there, whom I know from this time in Munich. And in this very moment, the owner of the shop explained to me that this is a very good wine. This is a case specially for a Marshal Milch. He will come and collect this.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this very moment, Marshal Milch walked in, see me. Ah, ah. I say, well, Milch, how are you? It was a rule. We never spoke to war criminals by rank. So it was not marshal or general or colonel, no-- first name only. And this I used to be in this time. So I, I, I I told him. I said, oh, Milch! What are you doing here? It's a wine waiting for you. He looked at me. He was white. I say, what you are doing here? I say, don't worry. Don't worry. I am not after you now. [LAUGHS] So he walked away. He forgot to collect his wine. He walked quick away. Maybe next day he came back. Well, and then I said, today is difficult for me to-- I remember some cases but names are gone off-- Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --my mind, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned earlier Kesselring.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kesselring. Well, he was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Why was he under my protection as a security officer in the general camps. He was walking in full uniform, you know, always with a adjutant, like he was looking like he was walking like a big bird, you know, in his, in his riding boots and-- well, it was fun, the generals--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- were you involved in interrogating him? No. No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was a-- Kesselring was not a war criminal, really. There was top-- the VIP war prisoners. War criminals, they were people from the SS and Gestapo, people from the-- I would say, administration on the occupied territories in Europe, yes. But the generals in-- well, some of them who were in the east front, they were involved. But most of them have been sent straight away to Russia for-- or to Poland for court.

speaker: interviewer
text: You, you said that the-- your involvement in the actual war crimes program began at the beginning of 1946. Is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you involved?

speaker: interviewee
text: One and half year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you located?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I left Germany, because I was a youngster, a youngster. I was 25. And I realized that this kind of work, this kind of mental-- I would say, stress, under which I was, changed my mentality, my [? psyche ?] in the way which I found-- thanks God at the right moment-- not in the proper direction. I, I was aware that longer I will do it, I will be closer to the mentality of the Gestapo people. So I received letter from my brother, calling me back to Poland and this. I was very close to my brother and this. And I decided to resign from the army and come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Were you in Germany the whole time? You were in Munich and Dachau the whole time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's where all the interrogations took place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the actual trials?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Both-- you gave evidence in two trials? You gave evidence in Boger--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and, and Boger. Boger was in Frankfurt a couple of years later.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was already in Poland, you see. I was sent to Frankfurt to give evidence. Because they-- they find out on the file my name.

speaker: interviewer
text: As the interrogator.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, that I interrogated him already. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just some facts-- what kind of housing did they give you as an interrogator? Were you in the military barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. No. I had a nice two-room in a villa in Dachau. On the ground floor was living a lady with two children whose husband was an SS, probably somewhere or killed in the war, or maybe arrested somewhere. And I had the first floor for me. And but I was eating always in the officer's casino, you know, in the officer's mess. And then this was-- I had a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: A jeep, A [INAUDIBLE], you know, to drive, when I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were given transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your comfort levels were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they provide a reasonable level of comfort for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But it [? was worth ?] it. You see, all American on the occupation of Germany had a very comfortable living condition. We had everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: The, the actual parameters of your work, how-- who gave you your orders?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I receive always for-- from the prosecution.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the prosecution counsel?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the prosecution. You see, they had-- especially, you see, was concerned myself-- I received cases of people from concentration camp. Because they could not write too much to me, because I was myself inside. So I know the story, how their life was going on, and so on. It was a different story when you had to do with people from the top Gestapo, from Berlin, and so on. This was not-- well, I, I had some time interrogating, like the Gestapo Muller, for example. I was involved, you see. And I-- it was very difficult job, because I had to switch from French into German, from German into French. Switch-- we are three, four of us interrogated at the same time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you would have more than one interrogator normally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was very seldom just one person interrogating?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sometime two, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And your, your main involvement was-- you were only involved with the American army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the American prosecution.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No other.

speaker: interviewer
text: No other group. OK. Can you tell us, you mentioned very briefly that your interrogations normally took place at night.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this the norm was-- or did different people have different ways? Were you-- was that your particular way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found this much easier. Because if you wake up somebody, psychologically, after one hour of sleep so he is a little bit not, not together, I would say, his brain is not working. So he's not defending himself so good. So it was easier. And well, and I keep him going till three, four o'clock in the morning. And then he was sent to his cell. And after two, three hours, he was waked up. And it was normal  day. So after two, three days, he was rather tired, not sleeping properly. Because during the day, they supposed not to sleep. They had no bed there. The bed have been locked. So you see, so maybe it was cruel. But it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: You had to do a job.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted the result.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And the actual facilities, the place, the spaces, et cetera, was it-- did the interrogations all take place in Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the actual Dachau camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't move them around to different places?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who? Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: The, the prisoners.

speaker: interviewee
text: The prisoners-- no, they have been in a prison, in cells in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them were in single, some of them two, three of them in the cell, and so on. But they were-- there was a strict prison, with guards looking on through on them, all day, day and night.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were-- they were-- under surveillance the whole time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no opportunity during the day to sort of--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They had no opportunity for nothing, nothing. They was-- I don't remember even walking time outside in the sun for them. No, no, no. They were very strict.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a high security.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was very strict.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very high security prison.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hard for them and strict.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. You were involved with-- you were actually in charge of the security for the prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for a certain category or certain [? fit ?] of prisoners, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you was-- it was-- no, as I told you, that were 41,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in charge of the security of this what they called hospital and general camp. It was half hospital. And the other half, there had been barracks with the VIP German people-- generals, marshals, ministers, and so on and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was that sort of like-- how many people were there-- were you involved with out of that 41,000? A quarter? Half?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. This was about, well, 200, 300.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was only a small part?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only the VIP.

speaker: interviewer
text: The VIP groups.

speaker: interviewee
text: Skorzeny, for example, or this man who liberated Mussolini was in this camp, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. OK.
